steps.title: Identify what the blank must connect
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) "Such interpretations are ultimately [blank]" and (2) "the object's gray coloration may be an incidental effect of radiation, solar wind, or collisions with other objects rather than evidence of its physical makeup." The word though acts as a conjunctive adverb (like however) that qualifies the first clause before the second clause begins. The required pattern is: IC, though; IC — a comma attaches though to the first clause, and a semicolon marks the boundary before the second independent clause.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice using punctuation boundary rules
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'speculative, though': No semicolon after though means the two independent clauses run together without a proper boundary — a fused sentence. Violates the rule that two independent clauses must be separated by a semicolon, period, or coordinating conjunction. ✗
  • Choice B — 'speculative, though;': The comma attaches the transition word though to the first clause, and the semicolon after though correctly marks the boundary before the second independent clause begins. Both punctuation marks serve their intended function. ✓
  • Choice C — 'speculative; though': The semicolon before though separates it from the first clause, making though appear to introduce the second clause as a subordinating conjunction — but then the second clause has no subordinate structure, creating an illogical boundary. Violates the rule governing placement of conjunctive adverbs. ✗
  • Choice D — 'speculative, though,': Two commas around though create a comma splice: the first comma cannot join two independent clauses, and the second comma does not establish a clear clause boundary. Violates the rule that commas alone cannot join two independent clauses. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
